architecture.summary: GraphToken is a parameter-efficient encoder that maps graph structures to soft tokens in an LLM's embedding space. A GNN (graph encoder) processes the input graph using learned or Laplacian positional node features and outputs fixed-dimensional token embeddings via readout (mean/sum pooling for graph-level tasks, per-node representations for node tasks, concatenated node pairs for edge tasks). These graph tokens are prepended to the text prompt embeddings and passed to a frozen LLM (PaLM 2), which generates answers. Only the GNN parameters are trained; LLM parameters remain frozen.
trained_components: projection layer to LLM embedding space

biblio.title: Let Your Graph Do the Talking: Encoding Structured Data for LLMs

claims.statement: GraphToken demonstrates significant improvements across node, edge, and graph-level tasks from the GraphQA benchmark, with up to 73% points improvement
On edge existence task, GraphToken achieves 0.738 accuracy compared to soft-prompt baseline of 0.544
On node degree task, GraphToken achieves 0.962 accuracy compared to soft-prompt baseline of 0.098
On cycle check task, GraphToken achieves 0.956 accuracy compared to zero-shot baseline of 0.760
Graph encoder parameters range from 17,152 to 198,788 parameters depending on architecture choice; projection layer adds approximately 11 million parameters total

Breaking equivariance via learned positional features (IDX) generally improves graph reasoning performance compared to Laplacian positional encodings (LPE), and combination of both types of features (LPE+IDX) proves particularly powerful for some encoders
Different graph convolution architectures (GCN, GIN, MPNN, HGT, MHA, Node Set, Edge Set) show varying performance across tasks with no single architecture dominating all tasks
Graph encoders trained on cycle check and triangle counting tasks generalize to unseen bipartiteness detection task, with attention-based methods performing best
